Author: Derek McBurney
Title: Should Developers Really Code with AI? A Dos and Don’ts Guide to Improve Your Developer Experience
Text:
Should Developers Really Code with AI? A Dos and Don’ts Guide to Improve Your Developer Experience
Love it or hate it, AI coding isn’t going away — using it wisely can improve your developer experience, but risks the opposite if you’re not careful.
I’ve been using AI in my coding for a while now.
I first switched on Github Copilot in 2023. What it offered at the time was fairly innocuous: code completion in your editor as you were typing away. Like an autocomplete on steroids.
Occasionally it would complete your code incorrectly. The worst instances being when it would do so in a way that was time consuming to detect. Mostly, however, it would complete pretty much exactly what you were intending to write.
You continued to have to know your stuff, and you didn’t have to change anything about how you worked. Copilot helped you type out your code faster and since it typically fills out your remaining line of code that you’re on, its use would never run away from you. You had eyes on everything. It was a nice little productivity boost that felt like a pretty safe augmentation for every coder.
Moral dilemmas of using AI aside, Copilot was a no-brainer to employ.
Then prompt-first, or “agentic” coding appeared. You could always turn to ChatGPT to prompt it for code ideas in a vacuum, but now editors had been designed to supply the context of your actual project code along with a user interface to merge in AI changes directly into your codebase. This made it powerful and convenient.
This was a new kind of workflow for programmers and one that I originally sat on the sidelines for.
But that workflow grew in adoption, and with it came all kinds of provocative claims that ranged from developers being made obsolete to AI producing complete junk. Day-by-day it felt increasingly irresponsible to let others shape how I should think about it, and not try it for myself.
So I tried it, and after a few months of using this approach on a number of personal and professional projects, I’ve got thoughts, some surprises, warnings and recommendations.
AI code creation is a paradigm shift that developers are programmed to hate
Developers are presented with a new interface for code creation: A prompt.
From there, it generates code and returns to something familiar for developers — diffs — a list of code additions, deletions and changes across project files to be reviewed.
This marks the largest and least satisfying change of prompt-based coding for a developer: the proportion of your time spent reviewing code instead of writing code increases. While reviewing code has always been an essential task in development, not just to work effectively within teams but also to debug issues — or simply remember what you did a month ago — developers seldom love doing it. Developers want to be unencumbered to write their own code.
Prompting AI to build code turns developers into release managers. That’s not what most developers signed up for.
And the other nagging feeling I can’t shake when using AI?
Its black-box, non-deterministic nature.
Developers are used to inputs and outputs: supply X data, and get Y result, consistently. The certainty of that is empowering. AI services don’t behave like that, by design, so relying on these incomprehensible services as part of your workflow when their offerings could change at a moment’s notice feels the opposite of empowering.
So it’s not hard to see why those sentiments, coupled with the moral quandaries of the whole AI industry, motivate many developers to forgo it entirely.
But AI coding is not going away.
Productivity can improve with AI, and surprisingly, so can the developer experience
So why have AI write code at all if it is such a drag on the developer experience and turns us all into soulless release managers?
Because it can have significant usefulness for developers.
It can scaffold out parts of your project, customized to your specific need, freeing more time for crunchy areas you need to focus more deeply on.
It can be a tool that you can bounce ideas off of, to quickly iterate and strengthen your approaches with.
It can help you write scripts to transform data or produce test information.
Some examples.
A hobby project I worked on, a conversation journal, makes recommendations of contacts you should reconnect with based on a formula that combines how frequently you talk with a person, with how long it was since the last time you recorded an entry with them. It’s a tricky balance: should a person you’ve spoken with once a year ago be recommended over someone you speak to every few days, but have missed talking to last week?
I wanted to put the time between a last conversation with a contact on a curve relative to all contacts, and I wanted to play with some data and get some simulated results, quickly. I got to work with AI, prompting it in my project:
Generate test data of contact entries and compare how that data set would be shown in the reachOutEntries component where we supply different standard deviations from the mean
AI wrote a quick script to generate some example contacts with simulated data of when conversations happened, then ran it through different standard deviation curves from my original formula to contrast which users would be recommended over others. It even offered meaningful insights into those results.
This saved me hours and hours of work, and within minutes allowed me to get at the heart of what was actually important in the build for me — fine-tuning the algorithm to achieve meaningful results.
In another example, this time a D&D spellbook builder of mine, I had AI write a script to convert markdown files of spell data into JSON files the application would use, then write the Javascript types for that data so that the rest of the application I was coding would be structured properly. This saved me time, avoided AI risking mangling my data since it would write a script to transform that data and would either work or not work and be easy to verify, and helped set up my project to use the data resiliently through my codebase.
These are just a couple of examples of very productive outcomes I’ve had coding with AI.
Using it has helped me build up experience to lean into its strengths and avoid its pitfalls.
It’s helping me become more ambitious — I’m following through on more of my ideas to see where they go rather than letting the grind of execution stop me on certain tasks before I start. In other words, the return on investment might make some tasks worth it that previously wouldn’t have been before.
And the most surprising and notable thing that’s happened as I’ve been coding with AI? I’m having more fun.
Bouncing back and forth with AI, iterating my contact recommendation algorithm? It truly felt like a next generation augmentation to my abilities as a developer, and was the motivation for writing this piece.
Originally sceptical of AI, coming to this realization was a big deal.
Yes, I’m doing more “release management” — reviewing code changes from AI — but I’m engaging AI at the times where this flow yields a boost so more of my time focusing on the areas that of my work that are novel, that have to feel perfect, that greatly benefit from close attention to detail. The satisfying stuff — the stuff where I actually provide value as a developer.
This is an objective improvement to my developer experience.
Using AI is the slipperiest of slopes, requiring a constant balance of thought and care
But of course, there’s a dark side to coding with AI, and it will immediately become apparent to every developer using it.
It can make you soft.
The code you get AI doing is code you’re no longer doing. That means you will not be practicing your craft with the same thought and effort. Your skills writing that particular code will wane.
Knowing this, be deliberate about what code you to get AI to write and not write. Focus it on tasks where you being your sharpest might not matter. And maybe you’re all better developers than me, but there’s always been syntax I can’t remember because I don’t need to use it much, a sign there are some areas of execution I’ve never needed to be my sharpest at. Simply knowing a pattern exists may be what’s useful for me. Having AI execute at that feels low risk to my talents.
In other areas, I have to remain at the top of my game. I’m constantly building pixel perfect websites, so I will not defer to AI write front-end, I do all my own styles.
It can switch from a tool to a crutch.
You will achieve useful results with AI, and it can start to become an addiction. You’ll start prompting it for more while giving it less, without even knowing it if you’re not careful.
GitHub added a feature to assign issues to their AI Copilot, so instead of working in a private setting to prompt AI and accepting, rejecting or changing its code recommendations as you see them in your editor, it applies code changes in the open in a GitHub pull request. Microsoft developers were then observed using this feature out in the open and quickly getting frustrated and prompting AI with requests like “It still doesn’t work”.
Obviously that’s terrible context to supply AI that will likely yield bad results. If you’re not getting a meaningful result quickly, break the addiction and get your hands dirty with code rather than endless prompts.
It can harm your learning.
It’s well documented that using AI can diminish your skills and ability to learn. You’re not forming the same memories as if you wrote the code yourself. So it came as a surprise for me to encounter times coding with AI where I found the opposite could be true — it was also tool for discovery and learning. But I had to make the choice to use it that way.
That meant actively reviewing the code it was producing rather than hitting accept, and looking up code I didn’t understand or hadn’t seen before.
“Oh there’s a HTMLDialogElement API now? I’ve never seen that schema vocabulary before, is it valid? Is that Svelte 4 or Svelte 5 syntax? Can I use that particular style in all browsers?”
Maintaining a healthy distrust of AI output and looking up anything that gives you pause is a great way to keep your learning up as well as ensuring what AI returns is useful and the basis for a good solution.
I hope you see the pattern here — how positively and negatively you’re impacted is directly a consequence of how you choose to work with AI.
You can very quickly devolve into helplessly prompting it for things you’re more suited for doing yourself, but only if you let it.
Whereas if you focus on employing it for specific tasks that cut down your time in meaningful ways, tasks that you don’t get a lot of benefit from practicing, or tasks where you have a crunchy problem you need to bounce ideas around, the sky is the limit.
Some more dos and don’ts
Try it. Earnestly try it.
If programming is your career, I believe it’s important to experiment with AI and see how it may work for you rather than adopt someone else’s opinion as your own and never know for yourself.
AI is simply too prolific to ignore.
There were those who stuck to their slide rules for their math, mourning their decline during the rise of electronic calculators. My mom is still on a wait-and-see approach to the Internet, happily doing all her banking in person. It’s possible these might have been the right choices for these individuals, but if the choice was only made because they never even considered the alternative, they’ll never know.
Understand it is both flawed and amazing, and these things don’t negate the existence of the other.
It seems like everyone has to pick a side when it comes to AI, but I find when a person speaks only from one extreme or the other, they begin to lose credibility. AI has far more utility than what the naysayers would have you believe and far more limitations and consequences than the boosters would have you believe.
Critics like to cite the times AI really fails at coding as justification to never use it. The GPS-enabled maps on my phone also give faulty directions from time to time that will result in a wave of expletives from this driver. But real time traffic conditions and near optimal routes in any jurisdiction I travel in? I’m not going back to paper maps.
There will be many who use AI to make pure, unmitigated slop. Likewise, there will be developers that use it to push further and achieve more exciting results with their time. Don’t let the existence of the former stop you from pursuing the latter.
Don’t play stupid games, or you will win stupid prizes.
If you make sweeping, unspecific and misguided prompts, you will get sweeping, unspecific, misguided results.
The thing with prompting AI is it does what you tell it. If you don’t realize your instruction isn’t a desirable approach, it seldom has the wherewithal to demonstrate that to you.
You need to know your stuff, and the more care you put into it and the more specific you get with it, the more useful the generated code is.
If you’re just looking get lazy, give it abstract prompts and use it to offload all of your thinking, you and your code will suffer for it. That’s fine for vibes, a small private project of no consequence, or demonstrating a concept, but for those who intend to be real developers capable of building production solutions, you have to use AI with rigour and thoughtfulness.
I flip-flopped writing this article, at times worried I sound too much like a shill for AI, other times worried I sound too cynical. Truth is, you can probably get a different answer from me any given day of the week. Heck, I don’t even like calling it AI as the “I” feels like a misnomer, but gave up fighting that battle. I cannot predict the future, but genuinely believe AI can be a boon, right now, for developers when used wisely. Let me know what you think, and good luck!
